question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed a didactic two-option choice for each of the three exercises (Hamming zip vs enumerate, RNA translate vs dict, Collatz recursion vs iteration). Every meaningful coding decision was framed as a user choice, though there were few follow-up questions beyond the initial fork.

question_quality.rationale: Each fork hit a genuine crux: idiomatic parallel iteration, a real correctness trap with str.translate silently passing invalid characters, and stack-depth concerns with recursive Collatz. These are exactly the subtleties worth surfacing.

scaffolding.rationale: Difficulty progresses sensibly from iteration idiom → validation strategy → algorithmic structure, and no answers are spoiled in the prompts. The distractor in option B for Collatz is fairly transparent though, and the Pair didn't explicitly elicit reasoning before confirming.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Conversation flows naturally with concise affirmations ('Spot on', 'Exactly right'), well-timed teaching nuggets (walrus operator, zip's truncation behavior, exact-error-message gotcha), and a clean closing synthesis tying the three decisions together.

effectiveness.rationale: All three solutions pass the official Exercism tests (9/9, 6/6, 9/9). The Pair even caught and fixed the trailing-period error message — something baseline didn't address since it never ran tests. Substantively better than baseline.

friction.rationale: Experiment is ~3x baseline duration (153s vs 50s) and 7 turns vs 2, but it delivered three solved exercises (vs one) with verified test runs and three substantive teaching moments. Per-exercise time is actually tighter than baseline with much higher pedagogical payoff.
